raw_text:
Contract Planning and Advance Approval

The Contract Planning and Advance Approval (CPAA) process allows for approval of the procurement strategy and pre-approval for contract entry.

The CPAA is used for approvals up to and including director general and regional director general, unless a procurement plan applies to the procurement.

The CPAA should not be used if there is insufficient known information about the expected results of the procurement action.

The CPAA process allows you to issue contracts, standing offers (SOs) and supply arrangements (SAs).

Contract planning and advance approval instructions

Contract planning is a critical step in procurement. You must complete the CPAA and explain the procurement strategy.

You should detail the following information in the CPAA , as applicable:

the proposed approval action to be taken (such as seeking approval or advance approval)

a description of the approvals requested (for example, approval to make advance payments or advance approval to firm up interim rates)

a description of the requirement including any options

the total estimated cost including all advance approvals, duties and taxes

for competitive procurements:

if you use tendering services, other public advertising methods or source lists

if you do not use tendering services, the rationale of this decision

for non-competitive procurements, the legal authority justifying the exception to competitive bidding

any policy issues (for example, socio-economic or accessibility considerations), policy deviations and associated recommendations

any risks related to the procurement, especially medium-high or high risks, and the proposed mitigation strategies

the evaluation criteria, solicitation method and selection methodology, including the pricing basis and mandatory and/or point rating criteria

the target dates for significant milestones (for example, solicitation publication, contract award, delivery schedule)

any other pertinent details for the approval authority

Advance approval and procurement execution

On receipt of the approval document, the approval authority will review the CPAA , either granting full approval, or identifying concerns and directing changes to the proposed strategy, the latter necessitating a CPAA resubmission.

Alternatively, the approval authority may decide to approve the procurement strategy but to not grant advance approval to enter into a contract. In which case, you must prepare and submit a Contract Request for approval before contract award.

Following CPAA approval, you will proceed with the procurement action. If the procurement action results in a significant change from the originally approved plan before the contract is awarded, the CPAA must be resubmitted. If there is no significant change, you will prepare the contract.

Contract Planning and Advance Approval resubmission

You must resubmit the CPAA when there is a significant change to a procurement strategy (e.g. a non-competitive requirement becomes competitive, or vice versa), or if the procurement action results in a significant change from the originally approved plan before the contract is awarded.

The following elements are required, as applicable, as part of CPAA resubmission :

the reasons why the procurement strategy has changed

any additional risks or changes to previously identified risks

the proposed mitigation strategies

an updated procurement complexity assessment

a new Procurement Risk Assessment (PRA) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - for Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) only), at the appropriate complexity level to accompany the procurement approval documents

any significant deviations from the initially approved procurement plan before contract award

the new CPAA documents to the designated approval authority, in accordance with sector and regional procedures

Contract summary

The contract summary is used pursuant to an approved CPAA to document the actions taken from the time the CPAA was approved up to the issuance of the contract, including offer evaluation results and contractor selection.
